exploration_log.aha_moment: http.StatusPermanentRedirect (308) is already in Go's net/http package. The fix is just a conditional: use 301 for GET/HEAD, 308 for everything else.
exploration_log.hindsight: Simple conditional; no supporting changes needed.

alternatives: Use 308 for all methods including GET/HEAD
key_decisions.decision: Use 308 for non-GET/HEAD methods; keep 301 for GET/HEAD
key_decisions.reasoning: 308 preserves the original method and body; 301 is acceptable for GET/HEAD since browsers already treat them as GET. 307 would be temporary which contradicts the semantic of cleaning a malformed path (which should be permanent).

solve_notes: In ServeHTTP's cleanPath redirect block, replaced the hardcoded `w.WriteHeader(http.StatusMovedPermanently)` with a conditional: 301 for GET and HEAD (safe since browsers follow these as GET), 308 for all other methods (preserves the original method and body on redirect).
task_text:
Fix Router.ServeHTTP clean path redirect using 301 for non-GET methods

In `mux.go`, when `cleanPath` produces a path different from the
request, `ServeHTTP` always responds with `http.StatusMovedPermanently`
(301). For POST, PUT, and DELETE requests, most HTTP clients convert
the redirected request to GET, losing the original method and body.
Fix the redirect to use 308 (Permanent Redirect) for methods other
than GET and HEAD so the original method is preserved.

relevant_preexisting_tests.reason: directly tests the 301 redirect behavior from cleanPath, exercising the code path that was changed

test_selection.test_query: Find tests that verify the behavior of Router.ServeHTTP and cleanPath redirect in mux.go

thrash_preventing_defs.name: cleanPath
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: understanding what cleanPath does (canonicalizes path, e.g. removes double slashes) helps confirm the fix context and that this is a permanent redirect scenario

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set is just ServeHTTP. The thrash_preventing set adds cleanPath so an agent understands why the redirect is permanent (canonical path normalization is a permanent operation) and confirms the fix context. Without it, an agent might wonder whether 307 (temporary) would be more appropriate.
